content.content:
content: "# Web Document

## Metadata

- title: Vehicle Warranty | Tesla Support
\
  - url: https://www.tesla.com/support/vehicle-warranty
- source: Tesla
- domain:\
  \ tesla.com

## Content

# Vehicle Warranty

A new Tesla vehicle is protected\
  \ by a New Vehicle Limited Warranty. That warranty includes several separate coverage\
  \ categories, each with its own scope and duration. Owners should identify the relevant\
  \ coverage category instead of assuming that every vehicle system follows the same\
  \ time or mileage limit.

Tesla notes that the current support page is for general\
  \ knowledge. Vehicle-specific warranty documents provided with the vehicle are the\
  \ authoritative source for a particular vehicle.

## Basic Vehicle Limited Warranty
\
  
The Basic Vehicle Limited Warranty covers the vehicle for 4 years or 50,000 miles,\
  \ whichever comes first.

Because the limit is expressed as time or mileage, reaching\
  \ either boundary ends that coverage period even if the other boundary has not yet\
  \ been reached.

## Supplemental Restraint System Limited Warranty

The Supplemental\
  \ Restraint System Limited Warranty covers the vehicle for 5 years or 60,000 miles,\
  \ whichever comes first.

This is a separate coverage category from the Basic\
  \ Vehicle Limited Warranty.

## Battery and Drive Unit Limited Warranty

The\
  \ Battery and Drive Unit Limited Warranty uses different limits that depend on model\
  \ and configuration.

For current vehicles listed by Tesla, the coverage period\
  \ is generally 8 years with a mileage limit that varies by model or trim. Current\
  \ listed mileage limits include 100,000 miles, 120,000 miles or 150,000 miles, whichever\
  \ comes first with the applicable time limit. Tesla also describes a minimum battery-capacity\
  \ retention threshold during the warranty period.

Because this coverage is separate,\
  \ a battery or drive-unit issue should not automatically be evaluated under the\
  \ Basic Vehicle Limited Warranty's 4-year or 50,000-mile boundary.

## Body and\
  \ Rust Limited Warranty

The Body Rust Limited Warranty covers rust perforation\
  \ caused by a defect in material or workmanship. Current coverage is 12 years with\
  \ unlimited miles. The page notes that vehicles purchased before February 1, 2019\
  \ do not have this Body Rust Limited Warranty.

## Pre-Owned Vehicle Limited Warranty
\
  
Tesla pre-owned vehicles purchased directly from Tesla receive any remaining portion\
  \ of the original 4-year or 50,000-mile Basic Vehicle Limited Warranty.

After\
  \ that original coverage expires, the Pre-Owned Vehicle Limited Warranty may provide\
  \ additional coverage for 1 year or 10,000 miles. If the Basic Vehicle Limited Warranty\
  \ had already expired before delivery of the pre-owned vehicle, the additional pre-owned\
  \ coverage starts from the delivery date.

The remaining original Battery and\
  \ Drive Unit Limited Warranty continues separately for pre-owned vehicles.

##\
  \ Parts, Body and Paint Repair Limited Warranty

Parts purchased directly from\
  \ Tesla, or purchased and installed by Tesla Service or a Tesla Collision Center,\
  \ may have separate repair-part warranty coverage.

Tesla describes a covered\
  \ failure as a complete failure or inability of a covered part to perform its designed\
  \ function because of a defect in material or workmanship under normal use. Gradual\
  \ loss of performance from normal wear and tear is not treated as a covered failure\
  \ under this repair-parts warranty.

Different part categories can have different\
  \ coverage periods. Owners should not assume that a replaced part follows the same\
  \ limit as the vehicle's Basic Vehicle Limited Warranty.

## Service Estimates\
  \ and Warranty Review

Before a service appointment, the Service team may review\
  \ appointment details, perform remote diagnostics and prepare an initial estimate.
\
  
Repairs expected to be covered by warranty should appear at $0 on the estimate.\
  \ Some repairs require an in-person inspection before coverage can be confirmed.\
  \ Those repairs may initially appear with a cost. If the repair later qualifies\
  \ for warranty coverage, Tesla states that the charges will be removed.

An initial\
  \ estimate is therefore not always a final coverage determination.

## Finding\
  \ Vehicle-Specific Warranty Details

Owners can view certain warranty details\
  \ in the Tesla app:

1. Open the Tesla app.
2. Scroll to the bottom of the app\
  \ home screen and select “Specs & Warranty.”
3. Open the “Warranty”\
  \ tab.

For a specific vehicle, the warranty document associated with that vehicle\
  \ should be used to confirm the applicable terms.
"
metadata: {}
title: Vehicle Warranty | Tesla Support
url: https://www.tesla.com/support/vehicle-warranty

content: '# Web Document


  ## Metadata


  - title: Extended Service Agreement Subscription | Tesla Support

  - url: https://www.tesla.com/support/extended-service-agreement

  - source: Tesla

  - domain: tesla.com


  ## Content


  # Extended Service Agreement Subscription


  Tesla offers an Extended Service Agreement (ESA) subscription for eligible vehicles.
  Eligibility depends on the vehicle and its status. The subscription is available
  for supported Tesla models and is subject to age, mileage, title and vehicle-history
  criteria. A vehicle with a branded title or one that has been determined to be a
  total loss is not eligible. Availability may also depend on whether a vehicle already
  has an active fixed-term ESA or, where applicable, passes an inspection.


  The Tesla app is used to check whether a specific vehicle is eligible. If the ESA
  subscription does not appear in the app, the vehicle may not meet the current eligibility
  criteria or the app may need to be updated. Owners with questions about vehicle-specific
  eligibility can contact Tesla Customer Support.


  ## Coverage Period


  ESA subscription coverage can continue until the vehicle reaches 8 years of age
  or 100,000 miles, whichever comes first, while the subscription remains in effect.


  Coverage does not begin simply because the owner has subscribed. The ESA coverage
  period begins when the vehicle''s Basic Vehicle Limited Warranty or Pre-Owned Vehicle
  Limited Warranty expires. The agreement therefore operates as later coverage rather
  than as a replacement for an original limited warranty that is still in effect.


  ## What the ESA Covers


  During the ESA coverage period, the subscription covers repair or replacement needed
  to correct a mechanical breakdown, or a covered part''s inability to perform its
  designed function, for most parts manufactured or supplied by Tesla under normal
  use.


  The subscription also includes Tesla Roadside Assistance during the ESA coverage
  period.


  Coverage terms are subject to the agreement and may change. Owners should review
  the current agreement terms associated with their vehicle.


  ## What the ESA Does Not Cover


  The ESA subscription has exclusions. Examples include maintenance parts and routine
  maintenance, appearance care, normal wear and tear, wheels and tires, and parts
  or accessories that have separate warranty coverage.


  Examples of separately covered systems include high-voltage batteries, drive units
  and safety or restraint systems. Those systems may be governed by their own limited
  warranties or separate service agreements.


  The ESA also excludes issues arising from certain events or conditions, including
  accidents, abuse and improper maintenance. Vehicles with branded titles or vehicles
  determined to be a total loss are not covered by the ESA subscription.


  Owners should review the Exclusions section of the agreement terms for the complete
  list of limitations.


  ## Battery and Drive Unit


  The standard ESA subscription does not extend the vehicle''s Battery and Drive Unit
  Limited Warranty. Battery and drive-unit coverage is handled separately. Depending
  on the Tesla model, the original Battery and Drive Unit Limited Warranty may continue
  for a different time and mileage period than the Basic Vehicle Limited Warranty.


  For that reason, an owner should first identify which vehicle system is involved
  and which coverage period is currently in effect before treating the ESA as the
  applicable coverage path.

  '
metadata: {}
title: Extended Service Agreement Subscription | Tesla Support
url: https://www.tesla.com/support/extended-service-agreement

Here’s what applies based on the current records:

- **Vehicle:** 2022 Tesla Model 3  
- **Concern:** Passenger-side door window intermittently fails to raise  
- **Cause recorded:** Mechanical-function failure under normal use; no external damage, collision, or maintenance-related cause identified  
- **Mileage at service checkpoint:** **49,820 miles**  
- **Service status:** **Coverage review pending**  
- **App estimate:** **$742.50**, but it is still provisional

### Which coverage to follow

1. **Have Tesla review it under the Basic Vehicle Limited Warranty first.**  
   The Basic Vehicle Warranty is generally **4 years or 50,000 miles, whichever comes first**. Your mileage is just below 50,000, but the time limit may be decisive depending on the vehicle’s original warranty start date.

2. **Your Extended Service Agreement does not normally overlap the Basic Warranty.**  
   Tesla’s ESA begins when the Basic Vehicle or applicable Pre-Owned Vehicle Warranty expires. It is intended as the later coverage path, not a replacement for an active original warranty.

3. **If the Basic Warranty has expired, ask Tesla to evaluate the repair under the ESA.**  
   This type of failure—an inability of a mechanical component to perform its function under normal use—appears consistent with the ESA’s general covered-breakdown description, subject to the agreement’s exclusions and Tesla’s diagnosis.

### What you should do now

Follow the **existing service request and let Tesla complete the pending coverage review**. Do not treat the $742.50 as a final customer-pay decision yet. Tesla states that repairs later confirmed as warranty-covered may have their charges removed.

You should specifically ask Service:

> “Please determine whether this window-regulator repair is covered under the Basic Vehicle Limited Warranty. If that warranty has expired, please evaluate it under my active Extended Service Agreement before I approve the estimate.”

You can also check the vehicle’s warranty start date and coverage details in the Tesla app under **Specs & Warranty → Warranty**.

Here’s what applies based on the current records:

- **Vehicle:** 2022 Tesla Model 3  
- **Concern:** Passenger-side door window intermittently fails to raise  
- **Cause recorded:** Mechanical-function failure under normal use; no external damage, collision, or maintenance-related cause identified  
- **Mileage at service checkpoint:** **49,820 miles**  
- **Service status:** **Coverage review pending**  
- **App estimate:** **$742.50**, but it is still provisional

### Which coverage to follow

1. **Have Tesla review it under the Basic Vehicle Limited Warranty first.**  
   The Basic Vehicle Warranty is generally **4 years or 50,000 miles, whichever comes first**. Your mileage is just below 50,000, but the time limit may be decisive depending on the vehicle’s original warranty start date.

2. **Your Extended Service Agreement does not normally overlap the Basic Warranty.**  
   Tesla’s ESA begins when the Basic Vehicle or applicable Pre-Owned Vehicle Warranty expires. It is intended as the later coverage path, not a replacement for an active original warranty.

3. **If the Basic Warranty has expired, ask Tesla to evaluate the repair under the ESA.**  
   This type of failure—an inability of a mechanical component to perform its function under normal use—appears consistent with the ESA’s general covered-breakdown description, subject to the agreement’s exclusions and Tesla’s diagnosis.

### What you should do now

Follow the **existing service request and let Tesla complete the pending coverage review**. Do not treat the $742.50 as a final customer-pay decision yet. Tesla states that repairs later confirmed as warranty-covered may have their charges removed.

You should specifically ask Service:

> “Please determine whether this window-regulator repair is covered under the Basic Vehicle Limited Warranty. If that warranty has expired, please evaluate it under my active Extended Service Agreement before I approve the estimate.”

You can also check the vehicle’s warranty start date and coverage details in the Tesla app under **Specs & Warranty → Warranty**.
